description: Complete Army combat arms and combat-adjacent MOS list with ASVAB requirements, OSUT duration, SOF pipeline paths, and career progression. Combat arms in the Army is defined as: Infantry (11), Engineers (12 — combat element), Field Artillery (13), Air Defense Artillery (14), Armor (19). Combat-adjacent includes Aviation (15), Special Forces (18), Ranger (75th), Civil Affairs (38), PSYOP (37), EOD (89), and Combat Medic (68W).

infantry_CMF11.description: I am the queen of battle. Infantry is the Army's primary ground combat force — the tip of the spear in every direct-action engagement. All other combat arms exist to support and enable infantry to close with and destroy the enemy.

mos_list.title: Infantryman
mos_list.description: I am the core infantry MOS. I fight on foot, operate rifles and machine guns, conduct raids and ambushes, and lead small-unit tactics. This is the most common path to Rangers, SF, and MARSOC (as a joint-service transfer).
mos_list.enlistment_option: 11X contract — Army assigns me to 11B (Rifleman) or 11C (Mortarman) after OSUT
sof_paths: Option 40 contract → Ranger Assessment and Selection Program (RASP) → 75th Ranger Regiment
After 1 enlistment → SFAS → Special Forces Qualification Course (SFQC) → Green Beret
Ranger Regiment → SFAS is fastest track to SF in practice
mos_list.civilian_value: Low direct transfer — law enforcement, security, law enforcement SOF programs
mos_list.first_person: My job is to close with and destroy the enemy. Every other MOS in this list either supports me or shares my battlefield. If I want Ranger or SF, this is the most direct and proven path.

mos_list.title: Indirect Fire Infantryman (Mortarman)
mos_list.description: I operate 60mm, 81mm, and 120mm mortars providing indirect fire support to infantry units. I can also attend the same SOF pipelines as 11B.
mos_list.first_person: I provide indirect fire support to my fellow infantrymen. I still qualify for every SOF pipeline available to 11B.

engineer_CMF12.description: Combat engineers are the Army's mobility, counter-mobility, and survivability force. We breach obstacles, clear routes of IEDs, build bridges, and demolish enemy structures. Combat engineers fight alongside infantry and are considered combat arms.

mos_list.title: Combat Engineer
mos_list.description: I construct and destroy obstacles, detect and neutralize explosives, perform route clearance, conduct breaching operations, and support combined arms maneuver. The 'Sapper' identity — the combat engineer's elite tier.

sapper_leader_course.description: The 'Sapper tab' equivalent of the Ranger tab for engineers. 28 days of intensive combat engineering, demolitions, land nav, small-unit tactics, and leadership. High attrition. Earns the Sapper Tab.
sapper_leader_course.note: Chain of command expects 12Bs to earn the Sapper tab before attending Ranger School

sof_paths: Sapper School → Ranger School → Ranger Regiment (Option 40 or volunteer)
SFAS → 18C (Special Forces Engineer Sergeant) — the SF engineer MOS
18C is one of the 5 SF specialty MOSs in an Operational Detachment-Alpha (ODA)
mos_list.first_person: My path to SF is through 18C — the engineer on an ODA. I can also go Ranger Regiment. The Sapper tab is my first major milestone.

mos_list.title: Bridge Crewmember
mos_list.description: I construct and operate tactical bridges and rafts for river crossings and gap crossings under combat conditions.
sof_paths: SFAS available as volunteer after serving in 12C

mos_list.title: Diver
mos_list.description: Combat diving MOS — underwater construction, demolition, and salvage. One of the most specialized 12-series MOSs.
mos_list.note: Extremely high ASVAB requirement. Direct pipeline to diving operations supporting SOF.

mos_list.title: Combat Engineer Senior Sergeant
mos_list.description: Senior NCO leadership MOS for experienced combat engineers. Career progression from 12B at E-7+.

field_artillery_CMF13.description: Field Artillery is the Army's indirect fire and fire support branch. I operate cannon, rocket, and missile systems, or I work as a forward observer calling in precision fires on the enemy. Artillery is considered combat arms.

mos_list.title: Cannon Crewmember
mos_list.description: I operate howitzers (M777 155mm, M119 105mm) providing indirect fire support. I load, aim, and fire artillery pieces in support of infantry and armor maneuver.
sof_paths: SFAS volunteer after completing first enlistment
13B can volunteer for RASP if physically qualified
mos_list.first_person: My artillery skills support every ground operation. If I want SF, I go the 18C or 18B route after SFAS.

mos_list.title: Fire Support Specialist (Forward Observer / FISTer)
mos_list.description: I am the forward observer — embedded directly with infantry, armor, and cavalry platoons at the front of the fight. I coordinate artillery, mortars, close air support (CAS), attack aviation, and naval gunfire. I use AFATDS digital fire control systems. This MOS is NOT rear-echelon — I am at the forward edge of the battle alongside infantry.
sof_paths: 13F in Ranger Battalion → most directly translates to SF 18F (Intel) or officer fire support paths
SFAS as volunteer → 18F or any 18-series after SFQC
JTAC-qualified 13Fs are highly valued in Ranger Regiment — actively recruited
mos_list.ranger_path: 13F is one of the few non-infantry MOSs actively recruited by Ranger Regiment due to fires and JTAC expertise. Option 40 and RASP2 available.
special_quals: JTAC (Joint Terminal Attack Controller) — coordinates close air support
mos_list.first_person: I call in the fires. I am embedded with the tip of the spear. My JTAC qualification makes me one of the most valuable non-infantry soldiers in Ranger Regiment and SF. If I want SOF, this is one of the strongest non-infantry starting points.

mos_list.title: Multiple Launch Rocket System (MLRS) Crewmember
mos_list.description: I operate the M270 MLRS and M142 HIMARS rocket systems — the Army's precision long-range strike assets. HIMARS has become one of the most strategically significant weapons of the 2022 Ukraine war and modern peer-to-peer conflict.
mos_list.first_person: I operate HIMARS. My skills are increasingly in demand as long-range precision fires become central to modern warfare.

mos_list.title: Field Artillery Radar Operator
mos_list.description: I operate counter-battery radar (TPQ-36, TPQ-37, Q-53) to detect and locate enemy artillery and mortar positions.

air_defense_CMF14.description: Air Defense Artillery protects the force and critical assets from aerial attack, missiles, rockets, and drones. As UAS (drone) threats grow in modern warfare, ADA has become more operationally prominent than any time since the Cold War.

mos_list.title: Air and Missile Defense Crewmember (SHORAD)
mos_list.description: I operate short-range air defense systems (Stinger, M-SHORAD, Avenger) protecting ground forces from helicopters, drones, and low-altitude fixed-wing threats.
mos_list.current_relevance: SHORAD has seen a massive renaissance due to drone warfare in Ukraine and peer-threat environments. M-SHORAD units are being rapidly expanded.
mos_list.first_person: My skills are some of the most relevant in modern warfare. UAS and counter-drone is the fastest-growing threat domain in any near-peer conflict.

mos_list.title: Patriot Missile Crewmember
mos_list.description: I operate the Patriot air and missile defense system protecting from ballistic missiles, cruise missiles, and aircraft.

armor_CMF19.description: Armor is the Army's shock and exploitation force. I operate main battle tanks (M1 Abrams) or serve as a Cavalry Scout — the Army's eyes and ears in reconnaissance and security operations.

mos_list.title: Cavalry Scout
mos_list.location: Fort Moore, GA (transitioning to Fort Knox, KY)
mos_list.description: I am the Army's reconnaissance and security specialist. My job is to find the enemy, not necessarily to destroy him — though I will if required. I operate Bradley Fighting Vehicles, Strykers, or HMMWVs. I am the first to make contact with the enemy, report, and shape the battlefield for the main force.

11B_vs_19D_honest_assessment.choose_19D_if: I want to understand the full battlefield, operate vehicles, and serve as the brigade's forward reconnaissance element. Stetsons and spurs. Unit esprit de corps is distinct.
11B_vs_19D_honest_assessment.choose_11B_if: I want maximum SOF eligibility and the most direct path to Rangers or SF. More slots, more schools, better promotion flow in some units.
11B_vs_19D_honest_assessment.reality: In modern counterinsurgency, 19D and 11B often do identical jobs. In near-peer war, scouts resume their true recon and security role.
11B_vs_19D_honest_assessment.both_can_do: RASP, SFAS, Ranger School, Airborne, Air Assault, Sniper School

sof_paths: RASP (Ranger Assessment and Selection Program) — same process as 11B
SFAS — same eligibility as 11B; will become 18D (medic), 18E (comms), or 18B/18C in SF based on testing
Army Reconnaissance Course (ARC) — professional certification course for scouts
mos_list.first_person: My radio is my primary weapon. I find the enemy first and report accurately. If I want SOF, every door open to infantry is open to me too.

mos_list.title: M1 Armor Crewman
mos_list.location: Fort Moore, GA (transitioning to Fort Knox, KY)
mos_list.description: I operate the M1A2 Abrams Main Battle Tank — one of the most powerful land weapons systems on earth. I serve as driver, loader, gunner, and eventually tank commander. The Abrams is 68 tons of firepower, protection, and mobility.
sof_paths: SFAS volunteer — will need a secondary MOS reclassification after SF selection
Less common SOF path than 11B/19D — tankers are valuable in heavy units
Some 19Ks serve in cavalry/recon roles compatible with Ranger Regiment
mos_list.civilian_value: Limited direct transfer but 22 weeks of OSUT builds exceptional discipline and mechanical/tactical skills. Veterans, mechanics, defense contractor roles.
mos_list.first_person: I am the hammer. My tank crew is a family of four. I won't have as many SOF opportunities as 11B, but I can still pursue Rangers or SF if I want to badly enough.

combat_adjacent_sof_enabling.description: These MOSs are not officially 'combat arms' but they fight alongside or within special operations units and have direct SOF pipelines.

mos_list.title: Special Forces Candidate (Direct Entry Contract)
mos_list.osut_and_pipeline_weeks: 10 weeks BCT + 3 weeks Airborne + SFAS (35 days) + SFQC (12-18 months depending on specialty)
mos_list.description: I enlist directly for the SF pipeline. If I fail SFAS or SFQC, I am reclassified to Army needs — potentially a non-combat-arms MOS I did not choose. High risk but fastest path if I qualify.
mos_list.risk: Failure = involuntary reclass (historically: 92G, 88M, 92A were common). 18X fail rate from contract to completion is significant.

specialties_after_sfqc.title: Special Forces Weapons Sergeant
specialties_after_sfqc.description: All weapons — U.S. and foreign. Direct action, raids, marksmanship training of partner forces.

specialties_after_sfqc.title: Special Forces Engineer Sergeant
specialties_after_sfqc.description: Demolitions, construction, breaching, route clearance, IED defeat. Pathway from 12B.

specialties_after_sfqc.title: Special Forces Medical Sergeant
specialties_after_sfqc.description: 50-week medic course — the most medically trained enlisted person in the DoD. Near-physician skill level. Pipeline from 68W.

specialties_after_sfqc.title: Special Forces Communications Sergeant
specialties_after_sfqc.description: All communications systems, clandestine comms, radio operations. Pathway from 25-series.

specialties_after_sfqc.title: Special Forces Intelligence Sergeant
specialties_after_sfqc.description: HUMINT, targeting, mission analysis, operational intelligence. Pathway from 35-series or 13F.

mos_list.first_person: If I am absolutely sure I want SF and I have the test scores and physical fitness, 18X is the most direct route. But failure means I go wherever the Army needs me.

mos_list.title: Combat Medic Specialist
mos_list.description: I am the primary battlefield medic. Combat Medics earn EMT-Basic certification and deploy embedded with every combat arms unit. 68W is the primary pipeline to 18D (SF Medical Sergeant) — one of the most elite medical roles in the world.
mos_list.18D_path: 68W → SFAS → selected for 18D track → 50-week Special Forces Medical Sergeant course (near-physician level medical training)
mos_list.sof_value: 18D is the most medically trained enlisted person in the entire DoD. SF medics perform surgeries, run clinics in denied areas, and train partner nation medics.
mos_list.first_person: If I want to be the 18D on an ODA — the team medic who can perform surgery in a mountain village — 68W is my starting point.

mos_list.title: 75th Ranger Regiment (Any MOS via RASP)
mos_list.description: The 75th Ranger Regiment is the Army's premier light infantry force. It is not an MOS — it is a unit assignment. Any qualifying MOS can attempt RASP (Ranger Assessment and Selection Program).

RASP_1.what_it_tests: Physical fitness, swim, land navigation, marksmanship, oral boards, psychological evaluation

RASP_2.note: Less physically intense, more leadership and judgment focused

mos_list.option_40_contract: Guarantees a RASP slot in your enlistment contract. Does NOT guarantee graduation or assignment.

ranger_tab_vs_scroll.tab: Earned by graduating Ranger School — open to most Army soldiers, not just Ranger Regiment
ranger_tab_vs_scroll.scroll: The unit patch of the 75th Ranger Regiment — only worn by those assigned there

mos_list.first_person: Ranger Regiment is where I prove myself before going to SF. Most SF soldiers I'll meet went through Regiment first. It is the best finishing school in the Army.

sof_decision_tree.description: My decision tree for Army SOF path — which MOS gives me the best foundation for which SOF unit?

if_I_want_rangers_first.path: OSUT → Airborne → RASP → Ranger Battalion → Ranger School (tab) → SFAS when ready

if_I_want_sf_directly.best_contract: 18X (risky) or 11B/13F → serve 1 enlistment → volunteer for SFAS
if_I_want_sf_directly.path: OSUT → serve in unit → SFAS (35 days) → SFQC by specialty (12-18 months) → ODA assignment

if_I_want_delta_force_eventually.best_path: 11B → Option 40 → Ranger Regiment → earn Ranger tab → SFAS → ODA → after 4+ years in SF → Delta Selection (OTC, 6 months, ~50% attrition)
if_I_want_delta_force_eventually.note: Delta selects almost exclusively from Special Forces and Rangers. Straight infantry rarely makes it.

if_I_want_combat_but_not_sof.note: All four put me in direct combat roles with real deployment cycles and meaningful career progression.
